content:
Of course. Here are 5 multiple choice questions on confidence limits, ranging from easy to challenging.

```json
[
  {
    "id": "q1",
    "question": "What is the primary purpose of a confidence interval?",
    "options": ["To provide a range of plausible values for an unknown population parameter", "To give the exact value of a population mean", "To show the standard deviation of a sample", "To determine the p-value for a hypothesis test"],
    "correctIndex": 0,
    "explanation": "A confidence interval uses sample data to estimate a range of values that is likely to contain the true, but unknown, population parameter (like the mean)."
  },
  {
    "id": "q2",
    "question": "If a 95% confidence interval for a population mean is calculated to be (48.2, 51.8) from a given sample, what is the correct interpretation?",
    "options": ["We are 95% confident that the true population mean lies between 48.2 and 51.8.", "95% of the sample data lies between 48.2 and 51.8.", "There is a 95% probability that the true population mean is between 48.2 and 51.8.", "The sample mean is 100% certainly 50.0."],
    "correctIndex": 0,
    "explanation": "Confidence refers to the long-run success rate of the method. It means that if we were to take many samples and build an interval from each, 95% of those intervals would contain the true population mean. It is not a probability about a single, fixed interval."
  },
  {
    "id": "q3",
    "question": "Which of the following changes would result in a narrower (more precise) confidence interval for a population mean, assuming all else remains constant?",
    "options": ["Increasing the sample size (n)", "Increasing the confidence level (e.g., from 95% to 99%)", "Increasing the sample standard deviation (s)", "Using a smaller critical value (z*)"],
    "correctIndex": 0,
    "explanation": "A larger sample size (n) reduces the standard error (s/√n), which is a key component of the margin of error. A smaller margin of error results in a narrower, more precise confidence interval."
  },
  {
    "id": "q4",
    "question": "You calculate a 90% confidence interval for the true proportion of users who click an ad and get (0.045, 0.089). Your boss says, 'So, the true proportion is definitely between 4.5% and 8.9%.' What is the best statistical response?",
    "options": ["The method used to create this interval has a 90% chance of capturing the true proportion in the long run, but this specific interval either contains it or it does not.", \"Yes, you are 100% correct. That's exactly what it means.\", \"Actually, it means there's a 10% chance the true proportion is outside this interval.\", \"No, it means 90% of user clicks fall within this range.\"],
    "correctIndex": 0,
    "explanation": "This addresses a common misconception. The confidence level describes the reliability of the estimation *process*, not a probability for a single calculated interval. The interval is fixed; the parameter is fixed. The probability that this specific interval contains the parameter is either 0 or 1, not 90%."
  },
  {
    "id": "q5",
    "question": "A data scientist is building a confidence interval for a mean with a very large sample (n=2000). The population distribution is unknown and heavily right-skewed. Which statement is most accurate?",
    "options": ["The Central Limit Theorem applies, so a z-interval for the mean is likely appropriate.", "Because the population isn't normal, a z-interval cannot be used under any circumstances.", "A confidence interval can only be built if the population follows a normal distribution.", "The large sample size makes the skew worse, invalidating the interval."],
    "correctIndex": 0,
    "explanation": "The Central Limit Theorem states that for a large enough sample size (typically n>30 is a common rule of thumb), the sampling distribution of the sample mean will be approximately normal, regardless of the shape of the population distribution. With n=2000, the condition is easily met."
  }
]
```
